title: Comparing Two Car Journeys
subject: physics
context: Two cars, A and B, start from rest at the same traffic light. The velocity-time graph below shows how the velocity of each car changes over the first 30 seconds. Car A accelerates uniformly to 20 m/s, then maintains this speed. Car B accelerates more slowly to 15 m/s, then also maintains a constant speed.

parts.question: What does the gradient of a velocity-time graph represent?

solutionSteps.text: The correct answer is C — the gradient of a velocity-time graph gives the acceleration.
A steeper gradient means a greater acceleration. A horizontal line means zero acceleration (constant velocity).
A common mistake is confusing this with the distance-time graph, where the gradient gives speed. Remember: d-t gradient = speed; v-t gradient = acceleration.

parts.question: Using the velocity-time graph: (i) What is the velocity of Car A at 10 seconds? (ii) What is the velocity of Car B at 10 seconds? (iii) Calculate the acceleration of Car A during the first 10 seconds.

readings.prompt: Velocity of Car A at 10 s
Velocity of Car B at 10 s

solutionSteps.text: (i) At t = 10 s, read across from Car A's line to the y-axis: velocity = 20 m/s.
(ii) At t = 10 s, Car B's line shows a velocity of 10 m/s (it is still accelerating — it reaches 15 m/s at t = 15 s, so at 10 s it is at 10 m/s from the straight line).
(iii) Acceleration = change in velocity ÷ time taken
A common mistake is confusing the gradient (acceleration) with the area under the graph (distance). Here we need the gradient of Car A's line.

parts.question: Calculate the total distance travelled by Car A in the first 20 seconds. Use the area under the velocity-time graph.

methodMarks.description: Correctly identifies area under graph = distance and splits into triangle + rectangle

solutionSteps.text: The area under a velocity-time graph gives the distance travelled.
Section 1 (0–10 s): triangle. Area = ½ × base × height
Section 2 (10–20 s): rectangle. Area = base × height
Total distance = 100 + 200 = 300 m
A common mistake is forgetting to split the area into shapes, or using the wrong shape. The accelerating section is a triangle; the constant-speed section is a rectangle.

parts.question: Between 15 and 30 seconds, Car B's line on the velocity-time graph is horizontal. What does this tell you about Car B's motion?

options.text: Car B is travelling at a constant velocity

solutionSteps.text: The correct answer is C — a horizontal line on a velocity-time graph means constant velocity (zero acceleration).
This is different from a distance-time graph, where a horizontal line means stationary. On a v-t graph, a horizontal line at 15 m/s means the car is moving at a steady 15 m/s.
A common mistake is choosing B (stationary). The car would only be stationary if the line was horizontal at v = 0 m/s, not at v = 15 m/s.

parts.question: Compare the journeys of Car A and Car B over the 30-second period. Which car travels further? Explain your reasoning using the velocity-time graph. [3 marks]

answerSchema.modelAnswer: Car A travels further than Car B in 30 seconds. The distance travelled is the area under the velocity-time graph. Car A has a larger area because it reaches a higher velocity (20 m/s vs 15 m/s) and accelerates more quickly (reaching top speed in 10 s vs 15 s). Car A's total area (triangle + rectangle) is approximately 500 m, while Car B's total area is approximately 337.5 m. Therefore Car A travels about 163 m further than Car B.

markSchemePoints.text: Car A travels further than Car B
Distance = area under the velocity-time graph
Car A has a greater area because it has a higher constant velocity (20 vs 15 m/s)
Correct or reasonable calculation/estimate of at least one car's distance

solutionSteps.text: Car A's distance: triangle (0–10 s) = ½ × 10 × 20 = 100 m; rectangle (10–30 s) = 20 × 20 = 400 m. Total = 500 m.
Car B's distance: triangle (0–15 s) = ½ × 15 × 15 = 112.5 m; rectangle (15–30 s) = 15 × 15 = 225 m. Total = 337.5 m.
Car A travels 500 − 337.5 = 162.5 m further than Car B.
The key reasoning is that the area under a v-t graph gives distance. A higher velocity and earlier acceleration both contribute to a larger area and therefore greater distance.
A common mistake is comparing only the final velocities without considering the full area under each line. The area takes into account both the speed and the duration.
